title: The Sword & Sorcery Saga - Warrior Witch of Hel, by Asa Drake (aka C. Dean Andersson)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=d-zJskCnHpI

Daniel (@dungeondive) enthusiastically reviews Warrior Witch of Hel by Asa Drake (C. Dean Andersson), the first book in the Blood Song trilogy. Bloodsong, a warrior killed by the sorcerer Nidhogg, pledges herself to the death goddess Hell and returns to the living world as a magical warrior with the power to summon skeletal hordes and command infernal magic. Joined by her old companions Thorpfin and Velgarth, and the young healer-witch Hold, she embarks on a quest to destroy Nidhogg and his War Skull—a magical totem powered by sacrificial victims that keeps him young and powerful.

Daniel (@dungeondive) praises the book's consistent action, strong female characters, and thoughtful handling of its nudity as a symbol of slavery rather than sexualization. The well-written prose avoids both purple excess and juvenile simplicity, while the party dynamics generate compelling banter and internal conflict. Though the heroes overcome challenges somewhat too easily, the character Jalna—brutalized by Nidhogg but fighting back aggressively—provides an arc of genuine struggle. Metal as heavy metal itself, this surprisingly literary action-packed adventure compels Daniel to immediately pursue the sequels.

----

What draws you to grimdark fantasy with darker themes, and would you be interested in exploring series that feature enslaved peoples rising up?
